title: The 8-Phase Workflow: How dotai Turned AI Coding Agents Into Spec-Driven Engineers
author: Niaz Bin Siraj
contents:
There's a dirty secret about AI coding agents. They're brilliant at writing code from prompts - but left unsupervised, they hallucinate scope, invent features nobody asked for, and produce code that "works" but doesn't match your intent. The problem isn't intelligence. It's structure.

With dotai v3.0.0, I'm introducing something I've been building toward since the project began: Spec-Driven Development - a structured 8-phase workflow that transforms AI agents from unpredictable code generators into disciplined engineers who implement exactly what you specified, and prove it.

The Core Problem: Agents Without Accountability

Consider the typical AI coding workflow today:

  1. You describe what you want in natural language
  2. The agent writes code
  3. You review it
  4. You find problems - wrong scope, missed edge cases, invented features
  5. You go back and forth correcting
  6. Eventually, something shippable emerges

This works for small tasks. But for anything substantial - a new feature with 12 tasks, multiple files, and interconnected logic - this conversational approach breaks down. The agent has no memory of what was agreed upon, no formal acceptance criteria, and no mechanism to verify its own work.

What if there was a specification artifact at every step, reviewed by a human, and enforced by automated evaluation? That's exactly what the 8-Phase Workflow does.

The 8 Phases at a Glance

The workflow is split into two halves: specification (Phases 1–4) and execution (Phases 5–8). Humans review at four critical checkpoints. No code is written until the spec is locked.

Phase 1: Initiate   → idea.md (you write this)
Phase 2: Specify    → requirements.md (AI, you review)
Phase 3: Decompose  → task files (AI, you review)
Phase 4: Plan       → plan + evaluation files (AI, you review)
Phase 5: Implement  → source code (AI)
Phase 6: Evaluate   → PASS/FAIL per task (AI, loops back to 5 on failure)
Phase 7: Review     → code-review.md (AI + you)
Phase 8: Sync       → knowledge/ updated (AI)

Walking Through Each Phase

Phase 1 - Initiate (Human Only)

You create a new feature and write idea.md. No structure enforced - just raw intent.

dotai sdd new user-authentication

Write what you want to build, why it matters, and any constraints you already know about. This is the most important document in the entire workflow. Everything downstream traces back to this file.

Phase 2 - Specify (AI Agent)

The Requirements Agent reads your idea.md and produces a structured requirements.md covering functional requirements, user flows, edge cases, and non-functional constraints. The critical rule: the agent does not invent scope. It only formalizes what you already implied.

/sdd-specify user-authentication

Human Review Checkpoint. This is the most critical review in the entire flow. A misaligned requirements document will silently corrupt every downstream phase.

Phase 3 - Decompose (AI Agent)

The Decomposer Agent breaks the work into discrete, ordered tasks - each scoped small enough for a coding agent to execute independently. Output: numbered task files like 01_project_setup.task.md, 02_data_model.task.md.

/sdd-decompose user-authentication

Human Review Checkpoint. Task order and granularity directly determine how implementation unfolds. If a task is too broad, the coding agent will make assumptions.

Phase 4 - Plan (AI Agent)

For each task, the Planner Agent produces two outputs: a plan file (exactly how to implement the task) and an evaluation file (the acceptance criteria - what "correct" looks like).

/sdd-plan user-authentication

Human Review Checkpoint (final before code is written). The evaluation files define the contract that Phase 6 will enforce. This is the moment where you define - before any code exists - exactly what "done" means for every single task.

Phase 5 - Implement (Coding Agent)

Now the coding agent works through the task list in order, one task at a time. It reads the .task.md and .plan.md files and implements accordingly. No creative interpretation. No scope expansion.

/sdd-implement user-authentication

Phase 6 - Evaluate (Evaluator Agent)

This is where SDD earns its keep. For each completed task, the Evaluator Agent reads the .evaluation.md file and checks the implementation against every acceptance criterion. It produces a .result.md with a PASS or FAIL verdict.

/sdd-evaluate user-authentication

If any task fails, the loop returns to Phase 5. The coding agent receives the failure details and reattempts only the failing task. No code advances beyond Phase 6 until every criterion is satisfied.

And with dotai's status reporting, you see exactly where you are:

Phase 5: Implement    ◐ 7/12 tasks implemented
Phase 6: Evaluate     ◐ 7/12 tasks evaluated (6 passed, 1 failed)

Phase 7 - Review (AI + Human)

Once all tasks pass evaluation, the Code Review Agent reads the entire codebase holistically - not task by task, but as a complete system. It looks for issues only visible at the system level: inconsistent patterns, redundant logic, security concerns.

/sdd-review user-authentication

Human Review Checkpoint (final gate). You read the review report. You decide what must be fixed before shipping.

Phase 8 - Context-Sync (AI Agent)

The Context Agent updates .ai/knowledge/ - refreshing memory files, gotchas, patterns, and codebase documentation to reflect everything built and decided during this feature cycle. The next development session starts with full awareness of what exists.

/sdd-sync user-authentication

Why This Works

The 8-phase workflow succeeds because it enforces three principles that conversational AI coding ignores:

1. Specification Before Implementation

Every piece of code traces back to an approved spec. The agent doesn't interpret your vague prompt - it follows a plan you already reviewed and corrected. This eliminates the single biggest source of AI coding waste: implementing the wrong thing.

2. Evaluation as a First-Class Concept

Acceptance criteria aren't an afterthought - they're written before implementation and enforced automatically. Failures trigger retries, not manual debugging sessions.

3. Human Gates at Critical Moments

The workflow doesn't remove humans from the loop - it puts them at the right points. Four review checkpoints, zero wasted implementation cycles.

Cross-Tool: One Workflow, Four Agents

Because dotai manages a single .ai/ source of truth and symlinks it to every tool, SDD commands work identically across Claude Code, Gemini CLI, GitHub Copilot, and Antigravity - from a single setup. 28 command files, generated automatically by dotai sdd init. Switch between tools mid-feature and the workflow doesn't skip a beat.

Getting Started

# 1. Install or upgrade dotai
npm install -g @nbslabs/dotai

# 2. Initialize your project
dotai init

# 3. Enable SDD
dotai sdd init

# 4. Create your first feature
dotai sdd new my-feature

# 5. Write your idea in .ai/sdd/my-feature/idea.md

# 6. Begin the workflow
/sdd-specify my-feature
/sdd-decompose my-feature
/sdd-plan my-feature
/sdd-implement my-feature
/sdd-evaluate my-feature
/sdd-review my-feature
/sdd-sync my-feature

The Bigger Picture

AI coding agents are a paradigm shift. But paradigm shifts without structure produce chaos. Test-Driven Development gave us tests-before-code. Spec-Driven Development gives us specs-before-agents - the same principle applied to the era of AI-generated code.

The 8-phase workflow doesn't slow you down. It eliminates the rework that currently makes AI coding unpredictable. Instead of three rounds of "that's not what I meant," you get one round of specification review and a machine that implements exactly what was specified.

Your intent → spec → tasks → plans → code → evaluation → review → knowledge. Eight phases. Zero ambiguity.
